changes.current: Always document the time the critical value was received, the time the provider was notified, the provider

changes.english: Anxiety/panic attacks, pain, fever, early salicylate poisoning, mechanical overventilation, hypoxia-driven tachypnea.
changes.current: Anxiety/panic attacks, pain, fever, early salicylate poisoning, mechanical overventilation, hypoxia-driven tachypnea.

changes.english: are the most accurate indicator of fluid status changes. A gain of 1 kg (2.2 lbs) = approximately 1 liter of fluid retention.
changes.current: are the most accurate indicator of fluid status changes. A gain of 1 kg (2.2 lbs) = approximately 1 liter of fluid retention.

changes.english: Assess for signs of stroke (FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 911). A-fib is the most common cause of cardioembolic stroke.
changes.current: Assess for signs of stroke (FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 911). A-fib is the most common cause of cardioembolic stroke.

changes.english: Below 7.35 = acidosis. Above 7.45 = alkalosis. Normal pH with abnormal CO₂ and HCO₃ = fully compensated.
changes.current: Below 7.35 = acidosis. Above 7.45 = alkalosis. Normal pH with abnormal CO₂ and HCO₃ = fully compensated.

changes.english: Calcium and phosphorus have an inverse relationship — when one goes up, the other goes down. Always check both values together.
changes.current: Calcium and phosphorus have an inverse relationship — when one goes up, the other goes down. Always check both values together.

changes.english: Cause water to shift from ECF into cells (ICF). Examples: 0.45% Half-Normal Saline (½NS), 0.225% Quarter-Normal Saline. Use for: cellular dehydration, hypernatremia. Caution: never give to patients with increased ICP (can worsen cerebral edema).
changes.current: Cause water to shift from ECF into cells (ICF). Examples: 0.45% Half-Normal Saline (½NS), 0.225% Quarter-Normal Saline. Use for: cellular dehydration, hypernatremia. Caution: never give to patients with increased ICP (can worsen cerebral edema).

changes.english: Causes include alcoholism, malnutrition, diuretics, diarrhea. Signs: tremors, hyperreflexia, tetany, seizures, cardiac arrhythmias (torsades de pointes). Nursing: IV magnesium sulfate (with calcium gluconate at bedside as antidote), monitor deep tendon reflexes, respiratory rate, and urine output.
changes.current: Causes include alcoholism, malnutrition, diuretics, diarrhea. Signs: tremors, hyperreflexia, tetany, seizures, cardiac arrhythmias (torsades de pointes). Nursing: IV magnesium sulfate (with calcium gluconate at bedside as antidote), monitor deep tendon reflexes, respiratory rate, and urine output.

changes.english: Causes include dehydration, diabetes insipidus, excessive sodium intake. Signs: thirst, dry mucous membranes, restlessness, seizures. Nursing: gradual rehydration with hypotonic fluids, monitor for cerebral edema, strict I&O monitoring.
changes.current: Causes include dehydration, diabetes insipidus, excessive sodium intake. Signs: thirst, dry mucous membranes, restlessness, seizures. Nursing: gradual rehydration with hypotonic fluids, monitor for cerebral edema, strict I&O monitoring.

changes.english: Causes include diuretics (furosemide), vomiting, diarrhea, alkalosis. Signs: muscle weakness, leg cramps, diminished reflexes, cardiac arrhythmias (flattened T waves, U waves, ST depression). Nursing: oral or IV potassium replacement (never push IV potassium), monitor ECG, assess digoxin toxicity risk.
changes.current: Causes include diuretics (furosemide), vomiting, diarrhea, alkalosis. Signs: muscle weakness, leg cramps, diminished reflexes, cardiac arrhythmias (flattened T waves, U waves, ST depression). Nursing: oral or IV potassium replacement (never push IV potassium), monitor ECG, assess digoxin toxicity risk.

changes.english: Causes include hyperparathyroidism, malignancy, immobility, excessive vitamin D. Signs: "bones, stones, moans, groans" (bone pain, kidney stones, confusion, GI symptoms). Nursing: hydration with NS, loop diuretics, calcitonin, bisphosphonates.
changes.current: Causes include hyperparathyroidism, malignancy, immobility, excessive vitamin D. Signs: "bones, stones, moans, groans" (bone pain, kidney stones, confusion, GI symptoms). Nursing: hydration with NS, loop diuretics, calcitonin, bisphosphonates.

changes.english: Causes include hypoparathyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, pancreatitis, renal failure. Signs: Trousseau's sign (carpal spasm with BP cuff), Chvostek's sign (facial twitch), tetany, seizures, prolonged QT. Nursing: IV calcium gluconate, seizure precautions, monitor ECG.
changes.current: Causes include hypoparathyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, pancreatitis, renal failure. Signs: Trousseau

changes.english: Causes include refeeding syndrome, antacid use, alcoholism, DKA treatment. Signs: muscle weakness, respiratory failure, confusion, rhabdomyolysis. Nursing: oral or IV phosphorus replacement, monitor respiratory status.
changes.current: Causes include refeeding syndrome, antacid use, alcoholism, DKA treatment. Signs: muscle weakness, respiratory failure, confusion, rhabdomyolysis. Nursing: oral or IV phosphorus replacement, monitor respiratory status.

changes.english: Causes include renal failure, ACE inhibitors, potassium-sparing diuretics, burns, crush injuries. Signs: muscle weakness, paresthesias, cardiac arrhythmias (peaked T waves, widened QRS, sine wave pattern). Nursing: cardiac monitoring, administer calcium gluconate (cardioprotective), insulin + dextrose, kayexalate, prepare for dialysis if severe.
changes.current: Causes include renal failure, ACE inhibitors, potassium-sparing diuretics, burns, crush injuries. Signs: muscle weakness, paresthesias, cardiac arrhythmias (peaked T waves, widened QRS, sine wave pattern). Nursing: cardiac monitoring, administer calcium gluconate (cardioprotective), insulin + dextrose, kayexalate, prepare for dialysis if severe.

changes.english: Causes include renal failure, excessive magnesium intake (antacids, laxatives). Signs: decreased DTRs, hypotension, respiratory depression, cardiac arrest. Nursing: stop magnesium, administer calcium gluconate, prepare for dialysis.
changes.current: Causes include renal failure, excessive magnesium intake (antacids, laxatives). Signs: decreased DTRs, hypotension, respiratory depression, cardiac arrest. Nursing: stop magnesium, administer calcium gluconate, prepare for dialysis.

changes.english: Causes include renal failure, tumor lysis syndrome, hypoparathyroidism. Signs: often asymptomatic; may cause symptoms of hypocalcemia (calcium-phosphorus inverse relationship). Nursing: phosphate binders with meals, dietary phosphorus restriction.
changes.current: Causes include renal failure, tumor lysis syndrome, hypoparathyroidism. Signs: often asymptomatic; may cause symptoms of hypocalcemia (calcium-phosphorus inverse relationship). Nursing: phosphate binders with meals, dietary phosphorus restriction.

changes.english: Causes include SIADH, heart failure, excessive hypotonic IV fluids, and diuretic use. Signs: confusion, headache, nausea, seizures (severe). Nursing: fluid restriction, monitor I&O, assess neuro status, administer hypertonic saline cautiously if severe (risk of osmotic demyelination if corrected too rapidly).
changes.current: Causes include SIADH, heart failure, excessive hypotonic IV fluids, and diuretic use. Signs: confusion, headache, nausea, seizures (severe). Nursing: fluid restriction, monitor I&O, assess neuro status, administer hypertonic saline cautiously if severe (risk of osmotic demyelination if corrected too rapidly).

changes.english: Chaotic, disorganized electrical activity — no effective cardiac output. Immediately defibrillate. CPR until defibrillator ready. Epinephrine + amiodarone per ACLS protocol.
changes.current: Chaotic, disorganized electrical activity — no effective cardiac output. Immediately defibrillate. CPR until defibrillator ready. Epinephrine + amiodarone per ACLS protocol.

changes.english: Complete AV dissociation — P waves and QRS complexes are independent. Wide QRS with slow ventricular rate. Emergency: transcutaneous pacing, atropine, prepare for permanent pacemaker.
changes.current: Complete AV dissociation — P waves and QRS complexes are independent. Wide QRS with slow ventricular rate. Emergency: transcutaneous pacing, atropine, prepare for permanent pacemaker.

changes.english: Comprehensive nursing study guides covering electrolytes, acid-base disorders, ECG interpretation, fluid balance, and critical lab values. Aligned with NCLEX and REX-PN exam blueprints.
changes.current: Comprehensive nursing study guides covering electrolytes, acid-base disorders, ECG interpretation, fluid balance, and critical lab values. Aligned with NCLEX and REX-PN exam blueprints.

changes.english: Confusion, muscle twitching, hand tremors, hypoventilation (compensatory — shallow breathing).
changes.current: Confusion, muscle twitching, hand tremors, hypoventilation (compensatory — shallow breathing).

changes.english: Consistent ECG analysis requires a systematic approach. Use the same sequence every time to avoid missing critical findings. The recommended approach: Rate → Rhythm → P waves → PR interval → QRS complex → ST segment → T waves.
changes.current: Consistent ECG analysis requires a systematic approach. Use the same sequence every time to avoid missing critical findings. The recommended approach: Rate → Rhythm → P waves → PR interval → QRS complex → ST segment → T waves.

changes.english: Consistent PR intervals with sudden dropped QRS complexes. More serious — can progress to complete heart block. May require pacing.
changes.current: Consistent PR intervals with sudden dropped QRS complexes. More serious — can progress to complete heart block. May require pacing.

changes.english: COPD, respiratory depression (opioids, sedation), pneumonia, airway obstruction, neuromuscular diseases (Guillain-Barré, myasthenia gravis).
changes.current: COPD, respiratory depression (opioids, sedation), pneumonia, airway obstruction, neuromuscular diseases (Guillain-Barré, myasthenia gravis).

changes.english: Critical (or "panic") lab values are results that fall outside the normal range to a degree that poses an immediate threat to patient safety. When a critical value is identified, the laboratory must notify the nurse or provider immediately, and the nurse must take appropriate action. Failure to act on critical lab values is a common cause of adverse patient outcomes.
changes.current: Critical (or "panic") lab values are results that fall outside the normal range to a degree that poses an immediate threat to patient safety. When a critical value is identified, the laboratory must notify the nurse or provider immediately, and the nurse must take appropriate action. Failure to act on critical lab values is a common cause of adverse patient outcomes.

changes.english: DKA, lactic acidosis, renal failure, diarrhea (loss of bicarbonate), toxic ingestions (methanol, ethylene glycol).
changes.current: DKA, lactic acidosis, renal failure, diarrhea (loss of bicarbonate), toxic ingestions (methanol, ethylene glycol).

changes.english: Dyspnea, confusion, headache, drowsiness progressing to lethargy, tachycardia.
changes.current: Dyspnea, confusion, headache, drowsiness progressing to lethargy, tachycardia.

changes.english: Electrolyte imbalances are among the most commonly tested topics on nursing licensing exams. Understanding normal ranges, clinical manifestations, and appropriate nursing interventions is essential for both exam success and safe clinical practice. Electrolytes are minerals that carry an electrical charge and are essential for nerve conduction, muscle contraction, fluid balance, and acid-base regulation.
changes.current: Electrolyte imbalances are among the most commonly tested topics on nursing licensing exams. Understanding normal ranges, clinical manifestations, and appropriate nursing interventions is essential for both exam success and safe clinical practice. Electrolytes are minerals that carry an electrical charge and are essential for nerve conduction, muscle contraction, fluid balance, and acid-base regulation.

changes.english: Electrolyte imbalances rarely occur in isolation. Hypokalemia often accompanies hypomagnesemia, and correcting the underlying cause is as important as treating the lab value.
changes.current: Electrolyte imbalances rarely occur in isolation. Hypokalemia often accompanies hypomagnesemia, and correcting the underlying cause is as important as treating the lab value.

changes.english: Expand the ECF without causing fluid shifts between compartments. Examples: 0.9% Normal Saline (NS), Lactated Ringer's (LR), D5W (initially isotonic but becomes hypotonic). Use for: volume resuscitation, dehydration, hemorrhage.
changes.current: Expand the ECF without causing fluid shifts between compartments. Examples: 0.9% Normal Saline (NS), Lactated Ringer

changes.english: Fluid and sodium restriction, administer diuretics (furosemide — monitor potassium), elevate HOB, daily weights, I&O monitoring, oxygen therapy if dyspneic, assess lung sounds frequently.
changes.current: Fluid and sodium restriction, administer diuretics (furosemide — monitor potassium), elevate HOB, daily weights, I&O monitoring, oxygen therapy if dyspneic, assess lung sounds frequently.

changes.english: Heart failure, renal failure, liver cirrhosis, excessive IV fluid administration, SIADH, excessive sodium intake.
changes.current: Heart failure, renal failure, liver cirrhosis, excessive IV fluid administration, SIADH, excessive sodium intake.

changes.english: help assess the kidneys' ability to concentrate urine. In dehydration: urine is concentrated (high specific gravity > 1.030, high osmolality). In fluid overload or diabetes insipidus: urine is dilute (low specific gravity < 1.005).
Hemorrhage, vomiting, diarrhea, excessive diuresis, burns, third-spacing, inadequate fluid intake.
changes.current: Hemorrhage, vomiting, diarrhea, excessive diuresis, burns, third-spacing, inadequate fluid intake.

changes.english: "I would like to request [specific orders: calcium gluconate, insulin/D50, kayexalate, repeat lab in X hours]."
changes.current: "I would like to request [specific orders: calcium gluconate, insulin/D50, kayexalate, repeat lab in X hours]."

changes.english: If HCO₃ is abnormal and explains the pH change, the primary disorder is metabolic. HCO₃ is a base — decreased HCO₃ causes acidosis.
changes.current: If HCO₃ is abnormal and explains the pH change, the primary disorder is metabolic. HCO₃ is a base — decreased HCO₃ causes acidosis.

changes.english: If PaCO₂ is abnormal and explains the pH change, the primary disorder is respiratory. CO₂ is an acid — elevated CO₂ causes acidosis.
changes.current: If PaCO₂ is abnormal and explains the pH change, the primary disorder is respiratory. CO₂ is an acid — elevated CO₂ causes acidosis.

changes.english: If the other system (respiratory or metabolic) is also abnormal and moving in the direction to normalize pH, compensation is occurring.
changes.current: If the other system (respiratory or metabolic) is also abnormal and moving in the direction to normalize pH, compensation is occurring.

changes.english: Improve ventilation (positioning, bronchodilators, incentive spirometry), administer supplemental oxygen cautiously in COPD patients, prepare for mechanical ventilation if severe, monitor respiratory rate and depth.
changes.current: Improve ventilation (positioning, bronchodilators, incentive spirometry), administer supplemental oxygen cautiously in COPD patients, prepare for mechanical ventilation if severe, monitor respiratory rate and depth.

changes.english: Irregularly irregular rhythm with no discernible P waves (fibrillatory baseline). Risk of atrial thrombus formation → stroke. Management: rate control (diltiazem, metoprolol), rhythm control (amiodarone, cardioversion), anticoagulation (warfarin, DOACs).
changes.current: Irregularly irregular rhythm with no discernible P waves (fibrillatory baseline). Risk of atrial thrombus formation → stroke. Management: rate control (diltiazem, metoprolol), rhythm control (amiodarone, cardioversion), anticoagulation (warfarin, DOACs).

changes.english: IV fluid replacement (isotonic fluids for volume expansion), strict I&O monitoring, daily weights (same time, same scale, same clothing), oral rehydration when tolerated, monitor vital signs for signs of improvement.
changes.current: IV fluid replacement (isotonic fluids for volume expansion), strict I&O monitoring, daily weights (same time, same scale, same clothing), oral rehydration when tolerated, monitor vital signs for signs of improvement.

changes.english: Know the "7/7 rule" for transfusion triggers — hemoglobin < 7 g/dL generally triggers RBC transfusion consideration. For platelets, < 10,000 = risk of spontaneous bleeding; < 50,000 = bleeding precautions needed.
changes.current: Know the "7/7 rule" for transfusion triggers — hemoglobin < 7 g/dL generally triggers RBC transfusion consideration. For platelets, < 10,000 = risk of spontaneous bleeding; < 50,000 = bleeding precautions needed.

changes.english: Kussmaul respirations (deep, rapid breathing — compensatory), confusion, fatigue, nausea, abdominal pain.
changes.current: Kussmaul respirations (deep, rapid breathing — compensatory), confusion, fatigue, nausea, abdominal pain.

changes.english: Lightheadedness, numbness/tingling (circumoral and extremities), muscle spasms, palpitations.
changes.current: Lightheadedness, numbness/tingling (circumoral and extremities), muscle spasms, palpitations.

changes.english: MONA (Morphine if needed, Oxygen if SpO₂ < 94%, Nitroglycerin, Aspirin 325 mg chewed). Activate cath lab for PCI within 90 minutes of arrival. Obtain serial 12-lead ECGs. Monitor for dysrhythmias.
changes.current: MONA (Morphine if needed, Oxygen if SpO₂ < 94%, Nitroglycerin, Aspirin 325 mg chewed). Activate cath lab for PCI within 90 minutes of arrival. Obtain serial 12-lead ECGs. Monitor for dysrhythmias.

changes.english: (normal: 275–295 mOsm/kg) reflects the concentration of solutes in the blood. It is primarily determined by sodium, glucose, and BUN. An elevated osmolality indicates concentrated blood (dehydration), while low osmolality indicates dilute blood (fluid overload or SIADH).
changes.current: (normal: 275–295 mOsm/kg) reflects the concentration of solutes in the blood. It is primarily determined by sodium, glucose, and BUN. An elevated osmolality indicates concentrated blood (dehydration), while low osmolality indicates dilute blood (fluid overload or SIADH).

changes.english: Normal sinus rhythm is the baseline against which all other rhythms are compared. Criteria: rate 60–100 bpm, regular rhythm, one P wave before each QRS, PR interval 0.12–0.20 sec, narrow QRS (< 0.12 sec). NSR originates from the SA node and represents normal cardiac conduction.
changes.current: Normal sinus rhythm is the baseline against which all other rhythms are compared. Criteria: rate 60–100 bpm, regular rhythm, one P wave before each QRS, PR interval 0.12–0.20 sec, narrow QRS (< 0.12 sec). NSR originates from the SA node and represents normal cardiac conduction.

changes.english: Nursing Study Guides — Free Exam Prep Resources
changes.current: Nursing Study Guides — Free Exam Prep Resources

changes.english: pH > 7.45 with HCO₃⁻ > 26 mEq/L. The body has excess bicarbonate or lost acid.
changes.current: pH > 7.45 with HCO₃⁻ > 26 mEq/L. The body has excess bicarbonate or lost acid.

changes.english: pH > 7.45 with PaCO₂ < 35 mmHg. The lungs are eliminating too much CO₂ (hyperventilation).
changes.current: pH > 7.45 with PaCO₂ < 35 mmHg. The lungs are eliminating too much CO₂ (hyperventilation).

changes.english: pH < 7.35 with HCO₃⁻ < 22 mEq/L. The body has excess acid or lost bicarbonate.
changes.current: pH < 7.35 with HCO₃⁻ < 22 mEq/L. The body has excess acid or lost bicarbonate.

changes.english: pH < 7.35 with PaCO₂ > 45 mmHg. The lungs are not eliminating enough CO₂.
changes.current: pH < 7.35 with PaCO₂ > 45 mmHg. The lungs are not eliminating enough CO₂.

changes.english: Potassium is the most commonly tested electrolyte. Remember: IV potassium must be diluted and infused slowly (max 10 mEq/hr peripherally, max 20 mEq/hr centrally). Never push IV potassium — it causes cardiac arrest.
changes.current: Potassium is the most commonly tested electrolyte. Remember: IV potassium must be diluted and infused slowly (max 10 mEq/hr peripherally, max 20 mEq/hr centrally). Never push IV potassium — it causes cardiac arrest.

changes.english: PR interval > 0.20 sec, every P wave conducts. Usually benign — monitor only.
changes.current: PR interval > 0.20 sec, every P wave conducts. Usually benign — monitor only.

changes.english: Progressive PR prolongation until a QRS is dropped. Often transient and asymptomatic. Monitor; usually no treatment needed.
changes.current: Progressive PR prolongation until a QRS is dropped. Often transient and asymptomatic. Monitor; usually no treatment needed.

changes.english: Prolonged vomiting/NG suction (loss of HCl), excessive antacid use, hypokalemia, diuretic use.
changes.current: Prolonged vomiting/NG suction (loss of HCl), excessive antacid use, hypokalemia, diuretic use.

changes.english: Pull water from cells into the ECF. Examples: 3% Saline, D10W, D5NS, D5LR. Use for: severe hyponatremia, cerebral edema (3% saline). Caution: give slowly; risk of fluid volume overload and phlebitis.
changes.current: Pull water from cells into the ECF. Examples: 3% Saline, D10W, D5NS, D5LR. Use for: severe hyponatremia, cerebral edema (3% saline). Caution: give slowly; risk of fluid volume overload and phlebitis.

changes.english: Rate > 100 bpm with normal morphology. Causes: fever, pain, anxiety, dehydration, hemorrhage, heart failure. Treatment: address the underlying cause — sinus tachycardia is a symptom, not a primary rhythm disorder.
changes.current: Rate > 100 bpm with normal morphology. Causes: fever, pain, anxiety, dehydration, hemorrhage, heart failure. Treatment: address the underlying cause — sinus tachycardia is a symptom, not a primary rhythm disorder.

changes.english: Rate < 60 bpm with normal P-QRS-T morphology. Common in athletes, during sleep, and with beta-blocker use. Treat with atropine if symptomatic (hypotension, altered mental status). Pacing may be needed for unresponsive symptomatic bradycardia.
changes.current: Rate < 60 bpm with normal P-QRS-T morphology. Common in athletes, during sleep, and with beta-blocker use. Treat with atropine if symptomatic (hypotension, altered mental status). Pacing may be needed for unresponsive symptomatic bradycardia.

changes.english: Remember "hypo = swells cells, hyper = shrinks cells." Never give hypotonic fluids to head injury patients (brain cells swell → increased ICP). Use hypertonic saline cautiously — too-rapid correction of hyponatremia causes osmotic demyelination syndrome.
changes.current: Remember "hypo = swells cells, hyper = shrinks cells." Never give hypotonic fluids to head injury patients (brain cells swell → increased ICP). Use hypertonic saline cautiously — too-rapid correction of hyponatremia causes osmotic demyelination syndrome.

changes.english: Replace chloride and potassium, administer anti-emetics, discontinue offending medications, monitor respiratory status for compensatory hypoventilation.
changes.current: Replace chloride and potassium, administer anti-emetics, discontinue offending medications, monitor respiratory status for compensatory hypoventilation.

changes.english: "Sawtooth" pattern of flutter waves, typically at 300 bpm with 2:1, 3:1, or 4:1 conduction. Ventricular rate is regular. Treatment similar to A-fib; cardioversion is often effective.
changes.current: "Sawtooth" pattern of flutter waves, typically at 300 bpm with 2:1, 3:1, or 4:1 conduction. Ventricular rate is regular. Treatment similar to A-fib; cardioversion is often effective.

changes.english: ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) requires immediate recognition and intervention. Look for ST-segment elevation ≥ 1mm in two or more contiguous leads. Territory localization: Inferior (II, III, aVF), Anterior (V1–V4), Lateral (I, aVL, V5–V6). Reciprocal ST depression in opposite leads supports the diagnosis.
changes.current: ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) requires immediate recognition and intervention. Look for ST-segment elevation ≥ 1mm in two or more contiguous leads. Territory localization: Inferior (II, III, aVF), Anterior (V1–V4), Lateral (I, aVL, V5–V6). Reciprocal ST depression in opposite leads supports the diagnosis.

changes.english: Tachycardia, hypotension, orthostatic hypotension, decreased urine output (< 30 mL/hr), concentrated urine (specific gravity > 1.030), poor skin turgor, dry mucous membranes, elevated BUN-to-creatinine ratio (> 20:1), weight loss (1 L = 1 kg = 2.2 lbs).
changes.current: Tachycardia, hypotension, orthostatic hypotension, decreased urine output (< 30 mL/hr), concentrated urine (specific gravity > 1.030), poor skin turgor, dry mucous membranes, elevated BUN-to-creatinine ratio (> 20:1), weight loss (1 L = 1 kg = 2.2 lbs).

changes.english: The body maintains blood pH between 7.35 and 7.45 through three buffering systems: the bicarbonate buffer system, the respiratory system (lungs), and the renal system (kidneys). Disruptions to this balance result in four primary acid-base disorders that nurses must recognize and manage.
changes.current: The body maintains blood pH between 7.35 and 7.45 through three buffering systems: the bicarbonate buffer system, the respiratory system (lungs), and the renal system (kidneys). Disruptions to this balance result in four primary acid-base disorders that nurses must recognize and manage.

changes.english: The lungs compensate quickly (minutes to hours) by adjusting CO₂ elimination. The kidneys compensate slowly (hours to days) by retaining or excreting bicarbonate.
changes.current: The lungs compensate quickly (minutes to hours) by adjusting CO₂ elimination. The kidneys compensate slowly (hours to days) by retaining or excreting bicarbonate.

changes.english: "The patient was admitted for [diagnosis]. Relevant history includes [medications, conditions]."
changes.current: "The patient was admitted for [diagnosis]. Relevant history includes [medications, conditions]."

changes.english: "The potassium came back at 6.8 mEq/L. The patient's current heart rate is [X], and they are [symptomatic/asymptomatic]. The ECG shows [findings]."
changes.current: "The potassium came back at 6.8 mEq/L. The patient

changes.english: The "ROME" mnemonic: Respiratory = Opposite (pH and CO₂ move in opposite directions). Metabolic = Equal (pH and HCO₃ move in the same direction).
changes.current: The "ROME" mnemonic: Respiratory = Opposite (pH and CO₂ move in opposite directions). Metabolic = Equal (pH and HCO₃ move in the same direction).

changes.english: Three or more consecutive PVCs at rate > 100 bpm. Wide QRS complexes. Pulseless V-tach: defibrillate immediately. V-tach with pulse: amiodarone, synchronized cardioversion if unstable.
changes.current: Three or more consecutive PVCs at rate > 100 bpm. Wide QRS complexes. Pulseless V-tach: defibrillate immediately. V-tach with pulse: amiodarone, synchronized cardioversion if unstable.

changes.english: to practice step-by-step acid-base analysis with clinical interpretation.
changes.current: to practice step-by-step acid-base analysis with clinical interpretation.

changes.english: Total body water comprises approximately 60% of body weight in adults (less in elderly and obese patients). This water is distributed between two main compartments: intracellular fluid (ICF, about 2/3) and extracellular fluid (ECF, about 1/3). The ECF is further divided into intravascular (plasma) and interstitial (between cells) compartments.
changes.current: Total body water comprises approximately 60% of body weight in adults (less in elderly and obese patients). This water is distributed between two main compartments: intracellular fluid (ICF, about 2/3) and extracellular fluid (ECF, about 1/3). The ECF is further divided into intravascular (plasma) and interstitial (between cells) compartments.

changes.english: Treat underlying cause (insulin for DKA, fluids for lactic acidosis), administer sodium bicarbonate if pH < 7.1, monitor potassium (shifts with acidosis correction), assess respiratory effort.
changes.current: Treat underlying cause (insulin for DKA, fluids for lactic acidosis), administer sodium bicarbonate if pH < 7.1, monitor potassium (shifts with acidosis correction), assess respiratory effort.

changes.english: V-fib and pulseless V-tach are shockable rhythms. Asystole and PEA are non-shockable. Know the ACLS algorithms for each.
changes.current: V-fib and pulseless V-tach are shockable rhythms. Asystole and PEA are non-shockable. Know the ACLS algorithms for each.

changes.english: Vomiting causes metabolic alkalosis (loss of stomach acid). Diarrhea causes metabolic acidosis (loss of bicarbonate from intestines). This distinction is commonly tested.
changes.current: Vomiting causes metabolic alkalosis (loss of stomach acid). Diarrhea causes metabolic acidosis (loss of bicarbonate from intestines). This distinction is commonly tested.

changes.english: "Water follows sodium" — remember that sodium problems are often water problems. SIADH = dilutional hyponatremia (too much water). Diabetes insipidus = hypernatremia (too little water).
changes.current: "Water follows sodium" — remember that sodium problems are often water problems. SIADH = dilutional hyponatremia (too much water). Diabetes insipidus = hypernatremia (too little water).

changes.english: Water moves between compartments by osmosis — from areas of lower solute concentration to higher solute concentration. Understanding this principle is essential for predicting fluid shifts with IV fluid administration.
changes.current: Water moves between compartments by osmosis — from areas of lower solute concentration to higher solute concentration. Understanding this principle is essential for predicting fluid shifts with IV fluid administration.

changes.english: NCLEX-style priority: match the intervention to the mechanism in the stem—orthopnea → reduce preload and orthopnea first; escalate oxygen when assessment shows need, not by default.
changes.current: NCLEX-style priority: match the intervention to the mechanism in the stem—orthopnea → reduce preload and orthopnea first; escalate oxygen when assessment shows need, not by default.

changes.english: Correct (A): Elevate the head of the bed. Orthopnea from decompensated HF worsens supine—increased venous return and pulmonary congestion raise work of breathing; raising the torso cuts preload and congestion before you add anything else. High-flow oxygen (B) is the classic trap: it treats hypoxemia, not positional overload. Unless the stem flags desaturation, oxygen after positioning follows assessment—boards expect the mechanism addressed first.
changes.current: Correct (A): Elevate the head of the bed. Orthopnea from decompensated HF worsens supine—increased venous return and pulmonary congestion raise work of breathing; raising the torso cuts preload and congestion before you add anything else. High-flow oxygen (B) is the classic trap: it treats hypoxemia, not positional overload. Unless the stem flags desaturation, oxygen after positioning follows assessment—boards expect the mechanism addressed first.
